explicit_text: I was born in Ibadan, Nigeria, where I had a good childhood despite the country being considered third-world. My dad worked as a lawyer or professor, and my mom was a secretary at an electronic firm there. Ibadan is where my early life and family roots are, before we moved to the United States when I was ten.

explicit_text: Nigeria is my home country, and although it's often called a third-world country, I still had a good upbringing there. My parents had respectable jobs—my dad was a lawyer or professor and my mom a secretary. We left Nigeria after winning the visa lottery when I was ten, hoping for a better life in America, but the reality was much harder than we expected.

explicit_text: We moved to the United States when I was ten after winning the visa lottery, landing in Minnesota on October 30. At first, the weather shocked us, and we soon realized America wasn't the perfect place we had imagined. My parents had to take jobs they never would have considered back in Nigeria, like working in nursing homes on weekends and holidays, which was tough for our family.

explicit_text: Growing up, I watched TV shows like Barney & Friends, which made me think America was a place where you could be anything you wanted. Those shows painted a picture of a happy, easy life that didn't match the reality my family faced after moving here. Barney was part of the media that shaped my early expectations of the American Dream.

explicit_text: My parents came to America chasing the American Dream, hoping for a better life, but it ended up failing them. They worked hard in difficult jobs and struggled to get ahead, with my mom unable to finish her degree and my dad only managing an associate's degree. I still hope the American Dream won't fail me, as I plan to go to law school and become a lawyer, showing that maybe it can still be achieved.
